reasoning: mtnc_register_settings() is hooked on admin_init (line 9), which fires for every authenticated admin-area request. The function at lines 79-115 gates the update_option() call only on check_admin_referer('mtnc_edit_post', 'mtnc_nonce') — a CSRF check — with no current_user_can('manage_options') capability check. WordPress nonces are NOT capability-gated at generation: any authenticated user (Subscriber, Contributor, Author, Editor) can call wp_create_nonce('mtnc_edit_post') programmatically, then POST lib_options[...] fields to admin.php or admin-ajax.php. The check_admin_referer() Referer header check is advisory only; the nonce value itself is sufficient. The settings page (line 7) uses add_menu_page(..., 'manage_options', ...) so non-admins cannot view the settings UI, but they do not need to: they can generate the nonce client-side and craft a direct POST. Upon a valid nonce + non-empty $_POST['lib_options'], update_option('maintenance_options', $lib_options) executes unconditionally, allowing a low-privileged authenticated user to overwrite all maintenance plugin settings — including toggling maintenance mode on to lock out front-end visitors. All AJAX handlers (wp_ajax_mtnc_dismiss_dialog, wp_ajax_mtnc_dismiss_notice) are wp_ajax_* only (no nopriv), so unauthenticated users cannot reach this; the attack requires a logged-in account of any role.
